coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of nitrogen-rich carbon dots decorated graphene oxide as metal-free electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Niu, Wen-Jun
Zhu, Rong-Hui
Yan-Hua,
Zeng, Hai-Bo
Cosnier, Serge
Zhang, Xue-Ji
Shan, Dan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, nitrogen-rich carbon dots decorated graphene oxide (N-Cdots/GO) hybrid, as a metal-free electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), is synthesized by a facile and low-cost hydrothermal treatment for the first time. Specifically, as a nitrogen-rich bridge-molecule, urea can trap free citric acid on planar planes, edges, and wrinkle sections of GO through amidation reaction in the initial stage. Then, polymer-like intermediates are in situ formed and carbonized to N-Cdots on GO. Owing to the simultaneous N-doping into both Cdots and GO characterized by XPS, as well as the formation of defective graphitic structures analyzed by Raman spectra, the as-prepared hybrid displays a remarkable electrocatalytic performance with more positive onset potential (0.13 V vs. Ag/AgCl) and high kinetic current density (up to 18.4 mA cm−2 at −0.70 V) comparable to commercial Pt/C catalyst. Furthermore, it demonstrates significantly higher catalytic efficiency (nearly 100% of four-electron ORR pathway) than those of N-Cdots or N-Cdots/GO mixture with an outstanding cyclic stability and superior methanol tolerance capability in alkaline solution.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: N-Cdots/GO hybrid as an electrocatalyst for ORR
The cyclic stability and durability of N-Cdots/GO hybrid
Synergic effect of N-Cdots and GO
The ORR kinetics performance of N-Cdots/GO hybrid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CVs of (A) bare GCE, (B) N-Cdots, (C) GO, (D) N-Cdots/GO mixture, (E) N-Cdots/GO hybrid, and (F) Pt/C electrodes in N2-saturated (curve a) or O2-saturated (curve b) 0.1 M KOH at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Chronoamperometry of Pt/C (curve a) and N-Cdots/GO hybrid (curve b) electrodes in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at −0.40 V for 11 h. (B) Chronoamperometric responses of Pt/C (curve a) and N-Cdots/GO hybrid (curve b) electrodes with 3 M methanol added. (C) CVs of N-Cdots/GO hybrid and (D) Pt/C in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution without (curve a) and with (curve b) 3 M methanol at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The TEM and HRTEM images of the as-prepared (A) GO, (B) N-Cdots, and (C, D, E, F) N-Cdots/GO hybrid.
(A) Raman and (B) FT-IR spectra of the GO (a), N-Cdots (b), N-Cdots/GO mixture (c) and N-Cdots/GO hybrid (d). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) XPS survey spectrum, high-resolution (B) C1s, (C) O1s peaks of GO (a) and N-Cdots/GO hybrid (b), respectively. (D) High-resolution N1s peaks of N-Cdots/GO hybrid. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) LSVs for the bare GCE (a), GO (b), N-Cdots (c), N-Cdots/GO mixture (d), N-Cdots/GO hybrid (e), and Pt/C (f) at 1000 rpm in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution at a rotating rate of 1000 rpm. (B) LSVs for the N-Cdots/GO hybrid electrode in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 at a series of rotation rates from 100 to 1000 rpm. (C) Koutecky−Levich plots (j −1 versus ω −1/2) of N-Cdots/GO hybrid at different potentials. (D) Dependence of the electron transfer number and (E) kinetic current density on the potential for N-Cdots, N-Cdots/GO mixture, N-Cdots/GO hybrid, and Pt/C electrode in the O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH. (F) Tafel plots of the N-Cdots/GO hybrid and Pt/C in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH (Tafel slope calculated by linear regression method). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic illustration the one-pot synthesis of nitrogen-rich carbon dots decorated graphene oxide hybrid.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of nitrogen-rich carbon dots decorated graphene oxide as metal-free electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sino-French Laboratory of Biomaterials and Bioanalytical Chemistry, School of Environmental and Biological Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, 210094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sino-French Laboratory of Biomaterials and Bioanalytical Chemistry
School of Environmental and Biological Engineering
Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Optoelectronics & Nanomaterials, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Micro & Nano Materials and Technology, College of Material Science and Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, 210094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Micro & Nano Materials and Technology
College of Material Science and Engineering
Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Univ. Grenoble Alpes, Département de Chimie Moléculaire, UMR CNRS 5250, 570 rue de la Chimie, CS 40700, 38058, Grenoble Cedex 9, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, nitrogen-rich carbon dots decorated graphene oxide (N-Cdots/GO) hybrid, as a metal-free electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), is synthesized by a facile and low-cost hydrothermal treatment for the first time. Specifically, as a nitrogen-rich bridge-molecule, urea can trap free citric acid on planar planes, edges, and wrinkle sections of GO through amidation reaction in the initial stage. Then, polymer-like intermediates are in situ formed and carbonized to N-Cdots on GO. Owing to the simultaneous N-doping into both Cdots and GO characterized by XPS, as well as the formation of defective graphitic structures analyzed by Raman spectra, the as-prepared hybrid displays a remarkable electrocatalytic performance with more positive onset potential (0.13 V vs. Ag/AgCl) and high kinetic current density (up to 18.4 mA cm−2 at −0.70 V) comparable to commercial Pt/C catalyst. Furthermore, it demonstrates significantly higher catalytic efficiency (nearly 100% of four-electron ORR pathway) than those of N-Cdots or N-Cdots/GO mixture with an outstanding cyclic stability and superior methanol tolerance capability in alkaline solution.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the increasing energy demands, rechargeable batteries have been regarded as a promising technology to meet the future energy requirements. However, the sluggish kinetics of oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) at the cathode in rechargeable batteries hinders their further development [1,2]. Although platinum-loaded carbon (Pt/C) is the most efficient electrocatalyst for those rechargeable devices as fuel cells and metal−air batteries [2–5], their high manufacturing costs and poor durability in relation to the methanol crossover effects are two major drawbacks hampering the widespread application [1,3,5–8]. Therefore, the development of low-cost, earth-abundant, durable and highly active ORR electrocatalysts to replace Pt-based electrocatalysts is paramount for the future rechargeable batteries technology.

Fortunately, nitrogen-doped graphene has been reported recently to exhibit remarkable electrocatalytic activity to the sluggish ORR at the cathode in fuel cells and metal-air batteries with low cost and high durability comparable to commercial Pt/C [9–13]. In general, two main synthetic routes are used to fabricate nitrogen-doped graphene materials. One is the post-treatment of GO with HNO3 or NH3 to introduce the N atom [12,14–17]. The other is the chemical vapor deposition (CVD) or pyrolysis of nitrogen-containing organic compounds to directly incorporate heteroatoms into the carbon backbone [18,19]. Unfortunately, owing to the inevitable aggregation of graphene sheets and low content of doped atoms, these pathways have obvious disadvantages including low efficiency, residual metallic impurity, and requirement of multiple organic precursors [20–22]. Consequently, it is urgent to develop a green, highly efficient, and environmentally benign route to synthesize N-doped carbon-based materials with high ORR catalytic activity.

Very recently, as a new emerging type of carbon-based nanomaterials, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-Cdots) or graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have also been proposed as potential metal-free catalysts for ORR [23–29]. They not only exhibit excellent electrocatalytic activity but also possess the advantages of low cost, long durability, and eco-friendliness [25–30]. Nevertheless, due to the low electrical conductivity of the Cdots or GQDs with high percolation threshold values, the enhanced electrocatalytic activity is limited. Curiously, whether the preconjugated or decorated graphene oxide with N-Cdots or N-GQDs has synergistic effect for electrocatalytic activities? Herein, inspired by this, for the first time, a rationally designed strategy has been developed for one-pot synthesis of N-Cdots decorated GO hybrid (Scheme 1 ). Specifically, as a nitrogen-rich bridge-molecule, urea can trap free citric acid on planar planes, edges, and wrinkle sections of GO through amidation reaction in the initial stage. Then, polymer-like intermediates are in situ formed and carbonized to N-Cdots on GO. The hybrid of N-Cdots/GO shows excellent ORR electrocatalytic activity with more positive onset potential and similar current density comparable to commercial Pt/C in alkaline media. Moreover, the prepared catalyst exhibits an outstanding cyclic stability and much better tolerance to methanol crossover than commercial Pt/C for ORR in alkaline solutions.

Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared from graphite flakes using the improved Hummers method [31]. Citric acid and urea were purchased from J&K Scientific Inc. (Shanghai, China). A commercially available platinum-loaded carbon (Pt/C) catalyst (20 wt% Pt loading on carbon black, Johnson Matthey) were used as received. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Double-distilled water was used throughout the experiment.

N-Cdots/GO hybrid was prepared by a hydrothermal carbonization protocol. In a typical one-step synthesis, 2.673 g citric acid, 0.90 g urea, and 0.05 g GO were dissolved into 30.0 mL of ultrapure water successively. Then, the homogeneous dispersion was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave chamber (50.0 mL) and heated to 180 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the reactors were cooled to room temperature. The obtained samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove excess reactants and uncombined N-Cdots. Finally, the resulting precipitate was dried in vacuum at 50 °C for 24 h to obtain N-Cdots/GO powders for further use. For comparison, N-Cdots was prepared using the same procedure except no GO was provided, while N-Cdots/GO mixture was prepared by mixing the appropriate amount of N-Cdots and GO together.

The morphology and size of N-Cdots/GO hybrid were analyzed using a TECNAI−12 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Philips) under an accelerating voltage of 300 kV. The crystalline structure and the surface functional properties of N-Cdots/GO hybrid were investigated by K−Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific). Raman spectra were recorded on an iHR550 Raman microscope (HORIBA scientific) with 532 nm solid laser as an excitation source. FT-IR spectra were measured on a pressed KBr pellet employing a TENSOR Model 27 FT-IR spectrometer. All electrochemical studies were performed with a conventional three electrode cell using Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) and a platinum wire as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. An aqueous catalyst solution dispersion of 20 μL (5.0 mg/mL, with 0.5 wt % Nafion solution) was transferred onto a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (diameter 3 mm) via a controlled drop-casting approach and dried in air for 12 h to serve as a working electrode. A CHI 660D electrochemical workstation (CH Instrument) was used for cyclic voltammetry (CV) and rotating disk electrode (RDE, 0.196 cm2, Pine Research Instrumentation, U.S.A.) tests. The scan rate of CVs was kept at 10 mV/s, while those for RDE tests were 5 mV/s. A flow of N2 or O2 was maintained over the electrolyte (0.1 M KOH) during electrochemical measurements for continued N2 or O2 saturation.

The electron transfer number (n) for the ORR in fuel cells is considered as the important factor in evaluating the performance of metal-free catalysts. The n of the electrocatalyst can be derived from the equation of the Koutechy–Levich plot (K–L):
(1)
1 j
=
1
j L
+
1
j K
=
1
B
ω
1 / 2
+
1
j K
in which, j is the current density, j
L is the limiting current densities, j
K is the kinetic current, and ω is the electrode rotating rate (rpm). The B could be obtained from the slope of the K–L plots. Therefore, n values could be obtained according to the Levich equation as below:
(2)
B = 0.2 n F
(
D
O 2
)
2 / 3
ν
− 1 / 6
C
O 2
where n represents the overall electron transfer number per oxygen molecule, F is the Faraday constant (96,485 C mol−1),
D
O 2
is the diffusion coefficient of O2 in 0.1 M KOH (F = 1.9 × 10−5 cm2 s−1), ν is the kinetic viscosity (ν = 0.01 cm2 s−1),
C
O 2
is the bulk concentration of O2 (
C
O 2
= 1.2 × 10−6 mol cm−3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-Cdots/GO hybrid as an electrocatalyst for ORR
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, a rationally designed strategy has been developed for one-pot synthesis of N-Cdots/GO hybrid as a metal-free electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). Therefore, the electrocatalytic performance of the hybrid was first examined by conventional three electrode cyclic voltammetry (CV) in 0.1 M KOH solution with N2 or O2 saturated. For comparison, bare GCE, N-Cdots, GO, N-Cdots/GO mixture, N-Cdots/GO hybrid, and commercial Pt/C catalysts (20%, Johnson Matthey) were tested under the same condition. The results of the CV measurements are illustrated in Fig. 1 . As expected, featureless CV plots are observed for both bare GCE (Fig. 1A, curve a) and N-Cdots (Fig. 1B, curve a) within the potential range from −0.8 to 0.2 V in N2-saturated KOH solution. However, compared with the cathodic peak around −0.458 V (vs. Ag/AgCl electrode) for the bare GCE (Fig. 1A, curve b) in O2-saturated solution, a weak and positive peak centered at −0.414 V is obtained for N-Cdots (Fig. 1B, curve b), suggesting its poor catalytic activity for ORR. In contrast to the inferior conductivity of N-Cdots, GO (Fig. 1C, curve a), N-Cdots/GO mixture (Fig. 1D, curve a), N-Cdots/GO hybrid (Fig. 1E, curve a), as well as Pt/C electrode (Fig. 1F, curve a) exhibit high conductivity with superior capacitive current in N2-saturated solution. Except for capacitive behavior, no noticeable reduction features are observed for all of them. Nevertheless, an obvious and strong reduction peak at −0.219 V is clearly observed for N-Cdots/GO hybrid (Fig. 1E, curve b) in O2-saturated solution, which is more positive than those of GO (about −0.391 V, Fig. 1C, curve b), N-doped GO (about −0.353 V, Fig. S1, curve a), N-Cdots/GO mixture (about −0.391 V, Fig. 1D, curve b) and close to commercial Pt/C catalyst (about −0.124 V, Fig. 1F, curve b). Moreover, the low electrical conductivity of the N-Cdots has been improved and the peak current density (j p) of N-Cdots/GO hybrid (j p = 1.150 mA cm−2) is significantly 1.4 times higher than that of N-Cdots/GO mixture (j p = 0.823 mA cm−2). Both the more positive potentials and higher j p explicitly indicate that the as-prepared N-Cdots/GO hybrid has a pronounced catalytic activity for ORR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The cyclic stability and durability of N-Cdots/GO hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: The well-known poor stability of commercial Pt/C catalyst, which originates from the aggregation of platinum nanoparticles after the oxidation degradation of carbon supports [2–5,32], is an obstacle hindering their application in fuel cells. Moreover, take the case of direct methanol fuel cells (DMFC), the commercial Pt/C catalyst exhibits weak durability in relation to using methanol as fuel [6,7,33]. It has been considered the methanol crossover effects are the main drawback limiting their application in DMFC. Therefore, the cyclic stability and durability of N-Cdots/GO hybrid and commercial Pt/C catalysts toward ORR were evaluated through chronoamperometric measurements at −0.4 V. The current-time (i-t) chronoamperometric response indicates that N-Cdots/GO hybrid decreases by only 6% over 11 h (Fig. 2 A, curve b), while the 20% Pt/C catalyst exhibits a sharp current decrease (30%) (Fig. 2A, curve a), suggesting an outstanding cyclic stability of our N-Cdots/GO hybrid for ORR in alkaline solutions.

In view of high stability, we would also expect N-Cdots/GO hybrid can be an efficient ORR catalyst in DMFC. As a result, we further test methanol tolerate ability of N-Cdots/GO hybrid and commercial Pt/C by adding 3.0 M methanol to 10 mL 0.1M KOH electrolyte. Upon introduction of methanol to the electrolyte, the prepared N-Cdots/GO hybrid exhibits no obvious change in the current density (Fig. 2B, curve b), indicating a remarkable crossover tolerance to the methanol. In contrast, owing to the methanol oxidation reaction, a sharp loss of 55% in activity is observed for the Pt/C catalyst (Fig. 2B, curve a). CV was also used to investigate the tolerance towards the methanol crossover effect of N-Cdots/GO hybrid and commercial Pt/C catalysts, respectively. The almost identical CV spectra indicate a good tolerance of N-Cdots/GO hybrid to methanol in agreement with the investigation of chronoamperometric measurements as exhibited above (Fig. 2C). However, the ORR peak (Fig. 2D, curve a) disappears and an anodic peak at −0.15 V (Fig. 2D, curve b) has emerged for Pt/C catalyst. The CV tests further confirm that N-Cdots/GO hybrid exhibits much better tolerance to methanol crossover than commercial Pt/C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergic effect of N-Cdots and GO
ce:section.ce:para: As previously mentioned, N-Cdots/GO hybrid greatly outperforms the GO, N-Cdots, and the mixture of them for ORR. It is well known that N-doping in carbon is an effect way to change the charge density and spin density of carbon atoms in carbon nanomaterials to enhance the electrocatalytic activity [9,12,34]. Moreover, as a new emerging type of luminescent nanomaterials, N-Cdots incorporation into the graphene layers may expose more active sites for ORR probably owing to their fascinating electrochemical properties [23,28,35]. With the aim to well investigate the origin of this characteristics, the low and high magnification TEM were carried out to characterize the morphology, size, and microstructure of the as-synthesized nanoparticles. Fig. 3 shows the representative TEM images of the GO, N-Cdots, and N-Cdots/GO hybrid, respectively. The GO is randomly compact and stacked together, showing uniform laminar morphology with micrometer size (Fig. 3A). The synthesized N-Cdots display a quasi-spherical shape, and monodisperse with particle size distribution in the range of 4 ± 2 nm (Fig. 3B). In contrast to “bare” GO and N-Cdots, fairly uniform N-Cdots with a diameter of 5 ± 2 nm are decorated on planar planes, edges, or wrinkle sections of GO (Fig. 3C). The formation of such small-sized monodisperse N-Cdots/GO hybrid is attributed to the presence of urea, as a bridge-molecule, which can trap free citric acid through amidation reaction in the initial stage. After that, polymer-like intermediates are in situ formed and carbonized to N-Cdots [36]. The structural details and the atomic lattice fringes could be observed clearly in HR-TEM. As illustrated in Fig. 3D, the N-Cdots decorated on GO and formed an interconnected nanostructure. The corresponding selected area HR-TEM image (Fig. 3E) exhibits small domains of defective graphitic structures and clear atomic lattice fringes of N-Cdots. As displayed in Fig. 3F, the lattice spacing (∼0.21 nm) agrees well with that of the (100) in-plane lattice spacing of graphite [27,37], indicating that the N-Cdots exhibit a high-crystalline graphitic structure in N-Cdots/GO hybrid.

As a result, the excellent electrocatalytic activity of N-Cdots/GO hybrid may be ascribed to the defects of graphitic crystal structure and synergic effect of N-Cdots. It is known to all that Raman spectroscopy is the most direct and nondestructive technique to characterize the structure defects of the carbon materials [11,35]. The ratio of peak intensities of raman spectra for D (breathing mode of sp2-hybridized carbon) and G (graphitic sp2-hybridized carbon) bands (ID/IG) reflects the extent of defects on graphene materials [35]. Therefore, the pristine GO, N-Cdots, N-Cdots/GO mixture, as well as N-Cdots/GO hybrid were collected by micro-Raman spectroscopy as Fig. 4 illustrated. It clearly shows that the value of N-Cdots/GO hybrid (ID/IG = 0.845, curve d) is lower than the GO (ID/IG = 1.08, curve a), N-Cdots (ID/IG = 0.999, curve b) or N-Cdots/GO mixture (ID/IG = 0.993, curve c). This indicates that partial sp2-hybridized carbon of GO is converted to graphitic sp2-hybridized carbon owing to the N-doping of graphene oxide layer at high temperature, and then, the small domains of defective graphitic structures are formed [11,35]. These results are in consistence with the investigation of TEM as exhibited above.

To investigate the synergic effect of the interconnected nanostructure thoroughly, FT-IR and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were further performed to analyze the elemental composition and nitrogen bonding configurations in N-Cdots/GO hybrid. The FT-IR spectra of GO (Fig. 4B, curve a) shows a broad peak centered at ∼3300 cm−1, which is attributed to stretching vibration of OH. The low-intensity peaks at ∼1724 and ∼1605 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CO and the bending vibrations of CH, whereas the peaks at 1212 and 1050 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibrational absorption of COH and CO, respectively. These characteristic absorption bands suggest that there are plenty of hydroxyl, epoxy, and carboxyl groups on the surface of GO [12,16]. However, different bands are found for the N-Cdots. As observed in curve b, a broad peak centered at 3450 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of OH and NH, the peak centered at ∼1050 and 760 cm−1 can be easily identified, which is attributed to the stretching vibrations and bending vibrations of CN [26]. All results indicate the Cdots are surrounded by amino group. As for the N-Cdots/GO mixture (curve c), the FT-IR spectra appears similar peaks as these characteristic absorption bands of N-Cdots and GO. Nevertheless, for N-Cdots/GO hybrid (curve d), an low-intensity peak at ∼3400 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of OH and NH, the small peaks at ∼1600 and 1400 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibrations of CO, COH, and the bending vibrations of CN. It is because a great deal of the O-containing groups on the surface of GO have been substituted by NH2 during the one-pot hydrothermal process. The FT-IR spectra suggests that the simultaneous reduction and N doping of Cdots and GO have been realized [23].

The survey spectrum of N-Cdots/GO hybrid (Fig. 5 A, curve b) shows three peaks corresponding to C, O and N elements, where the relative elemental contents is 59.5% for C, 29.1% for O, and 10.6% for N. This is in contrast to the XPS spectrum of GO (Fig. 5A, curve a), where only C and O elements are detected (65.9% for C, 32.9% for O). It indicates the citric acid and urea treatment introduces not only a large amount of oxygenated functional groups but also a certain amount of nitrogen-containing groups onto GO. Moreover, the greatly decreased contents of O in the XPS spectrum of N-Cdots/GO hybrid, which may be attributed to the reaction of urea with the reactive oxygen groups in hydrothermal treatment, also confirms that the N containing groups have been integrated into the graphitic structure. This is similar to the XPS spectrum of N-GO (Fig. S2, Supporting Information). To study chemical/electronic environments of neighbouring atoms comprehensively and reveal the potential electrocatalytic activities, the high resolution spectrum of C1s, O1s, and N1s were evaluated respectively. The C1s spectrum of N-Cdots/GO hybrid (Fig. 5B, curve b) can be deconvoluted into four different bands that correspond to CC (283.4 eV), CO/CN (284.3 eV), CN/CO (286.2 eV), and OCO (287.4 eV) functional groups. The slightly increased binding energy of N-Cdots/GO hybrid compared to the pristine GO (Fig. 5B, curve a) could be ascribed to the incorporation of N. The O1s spectrum of N-Cdots/GO hybrid (Fig. 5C, curve b) confirms these observations with two characteristic oxygen states of CO (530.1 eV) and COH/COC (531.6 eV), which have been observed in the case of C1s. The N1s spectrum of N-Cdots/GO hybrid (Fig. 5D) exhibited four relative nitrogen species of pyridinic-like N (398.2 eV), amino-like N (398.9 eV), pyrrolic-like N (399.7 eV), and graphitic-like N (400.5 eV) indicate the urea has been successfully doped on Cdots and GO. Meanwhile, it also reveals that N-Cdots/GO hybrid possesses a large number of edge-like graphitic structures. As the graphitic structure is known to be susceptible to oxygen adsorption even at low pressure [38], as a result, the prepared nanomaterials may exhibit remarkable ORR electrocatalytic activities. Therefore, the distinctive nanostructure of N-Cdots incorporation into the graphene layers favors the reactivity of the neighborly linked carbon atoms via alteration of the electronic structure as in the case of nitrogen-doped CNTs [33,39], and leads to the excellent electrocatalytic activity of the N-Cdots/GO hybrid than those of N-Cdots or N-Cdots/GO mixture.

In addition, the electrocatalytic activity of N-Cdots/GO hybrid with different N% toward the ORR process in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution was investigated. As Fig. S3 (Supporting Information) shown, the CVs exhibits similar electrochemical behavior for the ORR at all the N-Cdots/GO hybrid modified GCE. Although the potential of oxygen reduction exhibits a small negative shift, the reduction current increases with the increscent N% in N-Cdots/GO hybrid. This demonstrates that the N% in N-Cdots/GO hybrid does not significantly affect the electrocatalytic activity of N-Cdots/GO hybrid toward ORR. It is not the nitrogen content but the nitrogen bonding configurations in N-Cdots/GO hybrid determines the electrocatalytic activity of N-Cdots/GO hybrid toward ORR [11].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ORR kinetics performance of N-Cdots/GO hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: To well understand the essence of the satisfactory electrocatalytic activity of N-Cdots/GO hybrid, linear sweep voltammograms (LSVs) were recorded on a rotating disk electrode (RDE) in O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte at a rotating rate of 1000 rpm. For comparison, the ORR polarization plot of bare GCE, GO, N-Cdots, N-Cdots/GO mixture, and Pt/C (20%) electrodes were also given. As depicted in Fig. 6 A, the onset potential and half-wave potential (E1/2) of N-Cdots/GO hybrid derived from LSVs are 0.13 V and −0.152 V at a rotating rate of 1000 rpm, respectively. These potential values are remarkably more positive than those recorded for the N-Cdots, N-Cdots/GO mixture electrodes, and only slightly negative compared to commercial Pt/C. Furthermore, the current density (j) of N-Cdots/GO hybrid is 3.42 mA cm−2 at −0.8 V, higher than those of GO (j = 1.51 mA cm−2), N-Cdots (j = 1.82 mA cm−2), N-Cdots/GO mixture (j = 2.55 mA cm−2), and comparable with that of Pt/C (j = 4.08 mA cm−2). These results are consistent with the CV findings that N-Cdots/GO hybrid possesses much higher electrocatalytic activity toward ORR. It could be attributable to the faster reaction kinetics with a higher transferred electron number per oxygen molecule at the N-Cdots/GO hybrid modified electrode surface.

For further investigate the electron transfer involved in the ORR of the N-Cdots/GO hybrid, the RDE system at different rotating speeds was discussed in detail. As demonstrated in Fig. 6B and Fig. S4 (Supporting Information), the current density increases with enhancing rotating rates for all the catalysts owing to the shortened diffusion length at higher speeds [24,28,38]. As shown in Fig. 6C and Fig. S4 (Supporting Information), the Koutecky−Levich (K−L) plots present linear relationships between j −1 and ω −1/2 with a similar slope for all the catalysts, suggesting the first-order reaction kinetics towards the concentration of dissolved oxygen in the electrolyte solution [8,39]. As we known, the ORR in an alkaline solution occurs either via a four-electron (4e−) reduction processes where O2 is directly reduced to H2O or a two-electron (2e−) reduction pathway involving the formation of H2O2 as an intermediate [1–3]. Generally, the four-electron reduction pathway is preferred, because it provides a faster oxygen reduction rate [6,28]. Therefore, the electron transfer number (n) per oxygen molecule and kinetic current density (j
k ) were analyzed according to the K–L equations. Calculated from the slope of the K−L plots, the number of electrons transferred per O2 molecule varies from 2.5 to 3.2 over the potential range covered in the present study for N-Cdots and N-Cdots/GO mixture, suggesting a two-electron dominated ORR process on the electrode (Fig. 6D). However, the as-prepared N-Cdots/GO hybrid exhibits higher electron transfer numbers (
n ¯
=3.91) in a potential range of −0.3 to −0.7 V, representing an efficient four-electron (4e−) dominated ORR process similar to Pt/C catalyst (
n ¯
=3.96).

To further compare their catalytic activities, the ORR kinetic current density (j
k ) for all the catalysts are illustrated in Fig. 6E. The j
k value of N-Cdots/GO hybrid can reach 18.4 mA cm−2 at −0.70 V, which is nearly 2.5 times that of N-Cdots/GO mixture, 4 times that of N-Cdots, and comparable with Pt/C. In addition, as everyone knows, the larger the Tafel slope, the lower the catalytic activity [8,10,40]. Fortunately, the Tafel slope of N-Cdots/GO hybrid (∼114.5 mV/dec) is slightly larger than Pt/C catalyst (∼106.6 mV/dec) (Fig. 6F). As a result, a 4e− ORR process reveals the superior electrocatalytic activity of the N-Cdots/GO hybrid.

In summary, a one-pot synthesis and doping strategy was developed to prepare of N-Cdots decorated N-rich GO as a metal-free electrocatalyst for ORR. There are two reasons for N-Cdots/GO hybrid exhibit excellent electrocatalytic activity, one is the defects of graphitic crystal structure, and another is caused by synergic effect of N-Cdots. Practically speaking, owing to the N-doping of graphene oxide layer at high temperature, partial sp2-hybridized carbon of GO is converted and then, the small domains of defective graphitic structures are formed. Simultaneously, the distinctive nanostructure of N-Cdots incorporation into the graphene layers favors the reactivity of the neighborly linked carbon atoms via alteration of the electronic structure, and leads to the excellent electrocatalytic activity of the N-Cdots/GO hybrid than those of N-Cdots or N-Cdots/GO mixture. Therefore, the current work is expected to provide new insights in developing next-generation, highly efficient catalysts for the rechargeable devices as fuel cells and metal-air batteries.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21675086, 21175114, 51572128), National Key Basic Research Program of China (2014CB931702), Qing Lan project of Jiangsu Province, the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (30915015101), and a project founded by the priority academic program development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.08.002.
